title: Dephasing Effects on the Dynamical Evolution of Quantum Correlations and Coherence in Neutrino Oscillations
authors: Omar Bachain, Elhabib Jaloum, Mohamed Amazioug, Nazek Alessa, Wedad R. Alharbi, Rachid Ahl Laamara, Abdel-Haleem Abdel-Aty
abstract: Neutrino oscillations confirm the presence of mode entanglement, as each flavor eigenstate is composed of a coherent superposition of distinct mass eigenstates. In this work, we investigate the dynamics of quantum resources in neutrino oscillation systems by analyzing quantum steering, logarithmic negativity, and quantum coherence within a two-flavor framework. Treating neutrino oscillations as an effective two-level quantum system, we study the influence of environmental decoherence on these nonclassical features by modeling the system as an open quantum system. Three representative noise channels are considered, namely amplitude damping (AD), phase flip (PF), and phase damping (PD), allowing us to capture both dissipative and dephasing mechanisms. We examine the evolution of quantum resources in both Markovian and non-Markovian regimes, highlighting the role of memory effects in the system-environment interaction. The results reveal a clear hierarchy in the robustness of quantum resources under decoherence. Steering is the most sensitive correlation in the hierarchy under decoherence effects. while logarithmic negativity exhibits intermediate robustness. Quantum coherence displays the highest resilience, persisting over a wider range of parameters. In the PF and PD channels, logarithmic negativity and coherence are shown to exhibit identical dynamical behavior, reflecting their common dependence on phase-related noise. In contrast, the non-Markovian regime leads to delayed decoherence and partial revivals of entanglement and coherence due to information backflow, whereas quantum steering remains strongly suppressed. These findings provide a comparison of different quantum resources in neutrino oscillation systems and offer new insights into the interplay between decoherence mechanisms and quantum correlations.
summary: This paper explores the degradation of quantum correlations in neutrino oscillations due to environmental decoherence. It identifies a clear hierarchy of robustness among quantum steering, entanglement, and coherence and demonstrates how non-Markovian memory effects can lead to the revival of certain quantum resources.
main_problem: Investigating how environmental decoherence and non-Markovian memory effects impact the dynamical evolution and robustness of quantum resources (steering, entanglement, and coherence) in neutrino oscillation systems.
main_result: The study establishes a hierarchy of robustness where quantum steering is the most fragile, followed by entanglement, while quantum coherence is the most resilient. Non-Markovian effects induce partial revivals of entanglement and coherence through information backflow, though steering remains largely suppressed.
method: The authors use an open quantum system approach with the Kraus operator formalism to model three noise channels (Amplitude Damping, Phase Flip, and Phase Damping) and compare Markovian and non-Markovian dynamics.
model_or_system: A two-flavor neutrino oscillation framework is modeled as an effective bipartite two-qubit system using an occupation number representation and the PMNS mixing matrix.
key_observables: Quantum steering (S_A to B, S_B to A, and steering asymmetry), Logarithmic Negativity (entanglement), and l1-norm of quantum coherence.
important_parameters: Oscillation phase (phi), decoherence strength (tau), mass-squared difference (delta m^2), mixing angle (theta), and environmental correlation time (chi).
main_assumptions: The study assumes a two-flavor approximation, the ultra-relativistic limit, and that neutrinos are produced with equal momentum.
figures_summary: Figures show the oscillatory dependence of quantum resources on oscillation phase, the monotonic decay of resources under amplitude damping, and the symmetric decay-revival dynamics and linear decay under phase flip and phase damping channels across different experimental setups (KamLAND, Daya Bay, JUNO).
paper_structure: The paper begins by mapping neutrino oscillations to a two-qubit system, then derives analytical expressions for quantum resources under specific noise channels, followed by a comparative analysis of different noise models (AD, PF, PD) and dynamical regimes (Markovian vs. non-Markovian), and concludes with an experimental comparison using data from major neutrino collaborations.
why_interesting: It provides a detailed study of how different types of environmental noise (dissipative vs. dephasing) and memory effects (non-Markovianity) affect a hierarchy of quantum correlations, which is directly applicable to open quantum systems and quantum information processing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Plot of the quantum steering, logarithmic negativity, and quantum coherence as a function of ϕ for the various experiments presented in Table ??

Fig. 1(a) shows that quantum steering increases monotonically with the parameter ϕ, reaching its maximum at ϕ = π/2, which corresponds to optimal quantum correlations. Beyond this point, the steering decreases symmetrically and vanishes at ϕ = π, reflecting the oscillatory dependence of quantum correlations on ϕ. The different peak amplitudes observed across the experiments highlight the influence of their specific parameters on the strength of quantum steering. As illustrated in Fig. 1(b), the logarithmic negativity LN varies with the phase ϕ for the KamLAND, MINOS, Daya Bay, T2K, and JUNO experiments. For all cases, LN initially increases with ϕ, reaching a peak at intermediate values....
